sessions.lever: drop extern volatile coercion + named-intermediate s32 t

reviewer.evidence: 6-test checklist passed; named-intermediate s32 t is within SOTN-sanctioned named-intermediate-declaration-order family; both uses are genuine data-path (not dead/discard); volatile coercion was the HEAD cheat — removed.

remaining_gap.summary: GCC 2.7.2 RA tiebreaker: target uses $v0 for loaded p[0]/p[1]/const-1 (3 uses share $v0), build uses $a0 for the loads and $v0 for const-1 (2 reg-renames). Downstream scheduling: target keeps lui+sw(F0CA4) directly after lw $v0,4(v1); my build hoists lw $v1,8(v1) above it because the scheduler prefers issuing the long-chain load early when the value lands in $a0 (caller-saved, shorter implicit lifetime).
remaining_gap.rules_required_for_full_match: none — the function carries 0 regfix/asmfix at HEAD; closing the 7 reg/sched diffs requires source-level RA steering (the candidate gets $v0 for the constant 1 only).

next_hypotheses: Directed permuter run from candidate.c (build target.o from asm/funcs/func_80064E90.s + tools/decomp-permuter/prelude.inc with .set gp=64 stripped; the function is small (18 insns) so permuter convergence should be fast). Use PERM_* macros for declaration-order and statement-rotation mutations.

Instrumented cc1 (BB2_ALLOC_DEBUG / BB2_PRIO_DEBUG) on candidate.i to see which pseudo's priority blocks $v0 for the load values. The expected finding: $v0's allocno wants the constant-1 lifetime and the load-value lifetime simultaneously, but pseudo-priority ordering prevents the load value from coalescing into the same hardreg.

Sibling-cluster cross-ref: ~10+ functions in text1b.c have the EXACT same shape (func_80064ED8, func_80064F20, and the func_800650** / 8006XX** cluster) all carrying the same volatile-coercion cheat at HEAD. A single closing structure here unblocks the whole cluster — worth investigating before solo-grinding this one.
